attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old man is reviewed three months after being diagnosed with a duodenal ulcer. He was found to be Helicobacter pylori positive and was treated with esomeprazole, amoxycillin and clarithromicin (‘triple therapy’). After completion of the triple therapy, his dyspeptic symptoms returned. He has no other relevant medical history and takes no regular medications. He is an active smoker with a 10-pack-year history and drinks two cans of beer a day.

On examination he appears well with vitals within normal limits. The abdominal examination was normal except for mild, dull pain on palpation of epigastric region. A repeat endoscopy shows several shallow antral and duodenal ulcers and a rapid urease test is negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to prescribing salvage therapy, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Faecal antigen testing for Helicobacter pylori
attributes.explanation:
A refractory peptic ulcer is defined as an endoscopically proven ulcer greater than 5mm in diameter that does not heal after 8-12 weeks of treatment with a proton pump inhibitor. Peptic ulcer resistant to medical therapy warrants further diagnostic work-up and raises suspicion for Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (ZES), a rare neuroendocrine tumor that is characterized by ectopic secretion of gastrin. The diagnosis of ZES, requires demonstration of the inappropriate release of the gastrin and evidence of hypersecretion.

Fasting hypergastrinemia occurs in 97%–99% of patients with ZES, however, no absolute level of increase of FSG alone is diagnostic of ZES. A serum gastrin value greater than 10-times the upper limit of normal (1000 pg/mL) in the presence of a gastric pH below 2 is diagnostic of ZES.  The reason both tests are used in combination is because other conditions (i.e., atrophic gastritis caused by H. pylori) can cause hypergastrinemia due to disrupted regulation of acid production or the presence of hypo- or a-chlorhydria (resulting in reduced negative feedback and gastrin excess). Elevations of serum gastrin that are less than 10-fold the upper limit of normal are nonspecific and require further workup to exclude other causes. As such, determining the fasting serum gastrin level is the best initial test for diagnosing gastrinoma.

 The secretin stimulation test (SST) is used to differentiate patients with gastrinomas from other causes of hypergastrinemia (eg. as above less than 10-times upper limit of normal and gastric pH ≤2). Secretin stimulates the release of gastrin by gastrinoma cells, resulting in an elevation of serum gastrin levels for patients with ZES. This is an atypical response, as normal gastric G cells are inhibited by secretin which reduces the production of gastrin. This test is highly specific for gastrinoma acts as a confirmatory test. With this being said, it is not the best initial test in the diagnosis because it is not as sensitive as fasting serum gastrin.

Urea breath test and faecal antigen testing for H. pylori: Eradication of H. pylori may be confirmed by a urea breath test, faecal antigen test, or by upper endoscopy. This is typically performed four weeks or more after completion of antibiotic therapy and PPI therapy should be withheld for one to two weeks prior to testing. Both the UBT and FAT have high positive and negative predictive values regardless of H. pylori prevalence and infective status.

Helicobacter pylori serology: Serum antibody-based testing is cheap, effective, and quick to perform. This test has shown inconsistency in terms of sensitivity and specificity and a major drawback is the inability to differentiate between past and present H. pylori infections. This patient had a recent H. pylori infection which appears to have been eradicated but his serology results may still be positive and this is unlikely to help us with our diagnosis.
learningPoints: A serum gastrin value greater than 10-times the upper limit of normal (1000 pg/mL) in the presence of a gastric pH below 2 is diagnostic of ZES.
A refractory peptic ulcer is defined as an endoscopically proven ulcer greater than 5 mm in diameter that does not heal after 8 to 12 weeks of treatment with a proton pump inhibitor.
Standard therapy for H. Pylori is clarithromycin-based triple therapy which includes PPI, clarithromycin, and amoxicillin (or metronidazole).
references: Vakil. N., 2021. Approach to refractory peptic ulcer disease. In: UpToDate [online]. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/approach-to-refractory-peptic-ulcer-disease [Accessed 14 July 2021]

Bergsland, E. 2021. Zollinger Ellison syndrome gastrinoma. In: UpToDate [online] Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/zollinger-ellison-syndrome-gastrinoma-clinical-manifestations-and-diagnosis [Accessed 14 July 2021]
Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine, 20th ed. Fauci A, editor. New York United States of America: McGraw-Hill Education; 2018.

Raj P, Thompson JF, Pan DH. Helicobacter pylori serology testing is a useful diagnostic screening tool for symptomatic inner-city children. Acta Paediatr. 2017;106(3):470-477. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5303165/

Chey WD, Wong BC. American College of Gastroenterology Guideline on the Management of Helicobacter pylori Infection. Am J Gastroenterology. 2007;102(8):1808-1825. Available at: https://journals.lww.com/ajg/fulltext/2007/08000/american_college_of_gastroenterology_guideline_on.36.aspx

Ito T, Cadiot G, Jensen RT. Diagnosis of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome: increasingly difficult. World J Gastroenterol. 2012;18(39):5495-5503. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3482635/
